Credit-Based Pricing for SaaS: Why Usage-Based Billing Wins

Published March 21, 2026 · 7 min read

The Problem With Flat-Rate SaaS

Flat-rate subscription pricing made sense in 2010 when SaaS was primarily selling access to software features. You paid for a seat, you got the software, the cost to serve you was roughly the same regardless of usage.

That model breaks for AI-powered products where compute cost varies dramatically by user and task. A user who runs 10 AI operations per month costs you 100x less to serve than a user who runs 1,000. Flat-rate pricing forces you to either overprice for light users (killing conversion) or underprice for heavy users (killing margins).

# Flat-rate vs credit-based: the math
# Flat-rate problem: 80% of users use < 20% of value
# You're subsidizing heavy users and undercharging power users

# Credit-based aligns incentives:
# Light user (50 credits/mo):  pays proportionally less
# Power user (2,000 credits/mo): pays proportionally more
# Revenue scales with value delivered, not seat count

The compounding problem: heavy users evangelize your product most. If your pricing punishes them, you lose your best growth engine. Credit-based pricing rewards heavy users with volume discounts while protecting margins on your compute costs.

How Credit-Based Pricing Works

In a credit system, customers purchase a pool of credits that deplete as they consume value. One credit represents a defined unit of value — an API call, a computation, a generated artifact, a task completed. Credits are pre-purchased, so you collect revenue before delivery.

The key design decisions:

  • What does 1 credit represent? Define it clearly and tie it to a unit customers understand as valuable.
  • How do you price credits at each tier? Volume discounts incentivize higher-tier commitment.
  • What happens at zero balance? Hard stop, grace period, or auto-recharge — each has different conversion implications.
  • Do credits roll over? Monthly reset reduces revenue predictability but increases willingness to buy.

Implementation Guide

Build your credit system with three core components: a credit ledger (tracks balance and transactions), a gate middleware (checks balance before executing), and a webhook consumer (processes payments from NOWPayments or Stripe).

# Mekong MCU (Mekong Credit Unit) system
# 1 MCU = 1 unit of AI computation delivered

# Credit costs by command complexity:
mekong status          # 0 MCU  — read-only ops
mekong cook "..."      # 1-3 MCU — standard generation
mekong sales/prospect  # 3-5 MCU — research + generation
mekong annual          # 5 MCU  — multi-step analysis

# Billing logic:
# Deduct ONLY after successful delivery
# Zero balance → HTTP 402, no partial execution
# Monthly rollover on Pro+ plans

The gate middleware pattern is critical: check balance before execution, not after. Partial execution with a failed credit deduction creates bad UX and support tickets. Full atomic transactions — execute and deduct together, or neither.

# Pricing tier implementation
tiers = {
  "starter": {
    "credits": 200,
    "price_usd": 49,
    "price_per_credit": 0.245,
    "overage_per_credit": 0.35
  },
  "pro": {
    "credits": 1000,
    "price_usd": 149,
    "price_per_credit": 0.149,
    "overage_per_credit": 0.20
  },
  "enterprise": {
    "credits": "unlimited",
    "price_usd": 499,
    "price_per_credit": 0,
    "overage_per_credit": 0
  }
}
Tier Credits Price Per Credit
Starter 200 MCU $49/mo $0.245
Pro 1,000 MCU $149/mo $0.149
Enterprise Unlimited $499/mo $0

Case Study: Mekong MCU System

Mekong CLI uses MCU (Mekong Credit Units) as its billing primitive. Each MCU maps to one AI-powered task delivered successfully. Simple read operations cost 0 MCU. Complex multi-step analyses like annual strategy documents cost 5 MCU.

Critical design choice: deduct only on successful delivery. If the agent fails, the customer pays nothing. This aligns incentives perfectly — Mekong is motivated to deliver quality results because revenue depends on it.

The HTTP 402 response on zero balance prevents partial execution entirely. Customers who hit the limit get a clear signal to upgrade or purchase additional credits — not a degraded partial experience.

Conversion Optimization for Credit-Based Products

Credit-based pricing requires different conversion tactics than flat-rate subscriptions:

  • Free credits on signup: Give enough to experience real value, not just a demo. 20-50 credits is the typical range.
  • Low-balance notifications at 20%: Prompt upgrade before the user hits zero and churns out of frustration.
  • Usage-based upsell triggers: Users who consistently hit 80%+ of their monthly allocation are prime upgrade candidates.
  • Annual pre-purchase discount: 20% off annual credits vs monthly drives cash flow and reduces churn.

Try Mekong CLI — Pay Only for What You Use

Credit-based pricing. Free credits on signup. No flat fees for unused capacity.
